basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: al-Dhāriyāt — “The Scatterers” — begins with a fourfold oath by the agents of Omnipotence: the winds that scatter, the laden clouds, the gliding ships, the apportioning angels. Qushayrī grafts onto it a reading of the lovers: the winds carry the moaning of the distraught to the courts of Power, and the breath of nearness returns to the nostrils of the secrets. The sūra affirms the truth of the promise and the inevitability of Judgment, paints the godfearing who “slept little of the night” and implored forgiveness at dawn, and invites the reading of signs on the earth, in yourselves, and in the sky where your provision is. It recalls Abraham's honoured guests, the punishment of the peoples — Lot, Pharaoh, ʿĀd, Thamūd, Noah — and culminates on two summit-verses: “Flee to God!” (which Qushayrī unfolds into a ladder of flights: from ignorance to knowledge, from Him toward Him), and “I created jinn and humankind only that they may worship Me.”
themes: the oath by the agents of Power

versets.ar: وَالذَّارِيَاتِ ذَرْوًا
versets.tr: wa-dh-dhāriyāti dharwan
versets.trad: By those that scatter!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the Scatterers (al-dhāriyāt) are the winds. And he grafts onto it a mystical reading: among the winds are those laden with the cries of the lovers — they carry the moaning of the distraught to the courts of Omnipotence, and the breath of nearness then returns to the nostrils of the lovers' secrets, who find in it a respite from the assaults of passion. He cites: “I ask the breeze to bring me your fragrance, when it blows from your land.”

mots.racine: ذ ر و
mots.sens: by those that scatter

versets.ar: فَالْحَامِلَاتِ وِقْرًا
versets.tr: fa-l-ḥāmilāti wiqran
versets.trad: by those that bear a burden!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the Bearers of a load (al-ḥāmilāt) are the clouds. And in the inner register: some clouds rain in reproach of absence, announcing the assaults of estrangement and separation; the lovers perceive it by the light of their insight and cast themselves into imploring and prayer, seeking refuge from it.

mots.racine: ح م ل / و ق ر
mots.sens: those that bear a burden
mots.def: ḥāmilāt: the bearing clouds; wiqr: load.

versets.ar: فَالْجَارِيَاتِ يُسْرًا
versets.tr: fa-l-jāriyāti yusran
versets.trad: by those that run with ease!
versets.commentaire: The Gliders with ease (al-jāriyāt) are the ships. Qushayrī: just as the sailor sometimes takes the poor man aboard without fare, in hope of the ship's safety, so the poor in God hope to be carried in the bark of providence, on the seas of Power, when the waves rage about the vessel.

mots.racine: ج ر ي / ي س ر
mots.sens: those that glide with ease
mots.def: jāriyāt: the ships (that run); yusr: ease.

versets.ar: فَالْمُقَسِّمَاتِ أَمْرًا
versets.tr: fa-l-muqassimāti amran
versets.trad: by those that apportion a command!
versets.commentaire: The Apportioners of a command (al-muqassimāt) are the angels. Qushayrī: some angels descend to visit the people of union, or to console those of trial, or for a thousand affairs touching the people of this story; these folk question them about their states: have they news of their separation or their union? God swears by the Lord of these realities and by His power over them.

mots.racine: ق س م / أ م ر
mots.sens: those that apportion a command
mots.def: muqassimāt: the apportioning angels; amr: command.

versets.ar: إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَصَادِقٌ
versets.tr: innamā tūʿadūna la-ṣādiqun
versets.trad: Truly, what you are promised is true.
versets.commentaire: The answer to the oath: what you are promised is true. Qushayrī: the Real has promised the obedient Paradise, the penitent mercy, the saints nearness, the gnostics union; and to the afflicted He promised: “upon them blessings and mercy from their Lord.” They worry over the delay of the fair promise — yet God is gentle with the servants.

mots.racine: و ع د / ص د ق
mots.sens: what you are promised is true

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَوَاقِعٌ
versets.tr: wa-inna d-dīna la-wāqiʿun
versets.trad: Truly, the Judgment is inevitable.
versets.commentaire: The Judgment (dīn) — the recompense — will come about unfailingly (wāqiʿ). After the fourfold oath by the winds, the clouds, the ships, and the angels, God affirms the twofold certainty: the promise is true and the reckoning will take place. All creation attests, by its ordered service, the truth of the Day to come.

mots.racine: د ي ن / و ق ع
mots.sens: the Judgment is inevitable
mots.def: dīn: recompense; wāqiʿ: that will come about.

versets.ar: وَالسَّمَاءِ ذَاتِ الْحُبُكِ
versets.tr: wa-s-samāʾi dhāti l-ḥubuki
versets.trad: By the sky full of pathways!
versets.commentaire: A second oath: by the sky “of fair pathways” (dhāt al-ḥubuk). Qushayrī: the allusion is to the sky of tawḥīd, adorned with the sun of gnosis, the moon of love, and the stars of nearness: you hold, concerning this Path, divergent discourses. The streaked firmament becomes an image of gnosis adorned with its inner stars.

mots.racine: س م و / ح ب ك
mots.sens: by the sky of fair pathways

versets.ar: إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُّخْتَلِفٍ
versets.tr: innakum la-fī qawlin mukhtalifin
versets.trad: You are in divergent discourse;
versets.commentaire: The answer: you are in contradictory speech. Qushayrī: about Muḥammad, one says: a magician, another: possessed, a third: a poet. And he transposes: before this Path, one denies the way, another contests its people, imagining they fall short of the Law, a third, narrow, does not emerge from the strictness of common servitude and knows nothing of how God distinguishes His saints by the sublime states.

mots.racine: ق و ل / خ ل ف
mots.sens: you are in divergent discourse

versets.ar: يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ
versets.tr: yuʾfaku ʿanhu man ufika
versets.trad: turned away from it is whoever is turned away.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: turned away from him (the Prophet, or the Truth) is the one who was turned away — that is, the one whom the prior decree has deflected. Those who barred people from approaching him by calling him mad were only fulfilling, unknowingly, the primordial allotment: one turns from the light only according to what was written.

mots.racine: أ ف ك
mots.sens: turned from it is whoever was turned
mots.def: ufika: to be deflected from truth.

versets.ar: قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ
versets.tr: qutila l-kharrāṣūna
versets.trad: Cursed be the conjecturers,
versets.commentaire: “Cursed be the conjecturers” (al-kharrāṣūn). Qushayrī: the cursed liars who, in the flood of error and the darkness of ignorance, are distracted and heedless. The kharrāṣ is one who speaks by supposition, without knowledge: his vain word about God and His Truth condemns him.

mots.racine: ق ت ل / خ ر ص
mots.sens: cursed be the conjecturers
mots.def: kharrāṣ: who speaks by conjecture, a liar.

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ سَاهُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna hum fī ghamratin sāhūna
versets.trad: who are plunged in heedlessness!
versets.commentaire: They are “in a flood, distracted” (fī ghamra sāhūn). Qushayrī: in the flood of error and the darkness of ignorance, they are forgetful and frivolous. The ghamra is the deep water that submerges: they are drowned in their inadvertence, unable to perceive the Day that approaches.

mots.racine: غ م ر / س ه و
mots.sens: in a flood, distracted

versets.ar: يَسْأَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ
versets.tr: yasʾalūna ayyāna yawmu d-dīni
versets.trad: They ask: “When is the Day of Judgment?”
versets.commentaire: They ask in defiance: “When then the Day of Judgment?” Qushayrī: because they deny it, their souls do not settle to it. The question is not inquiry but mockery: eager to see come what they deny, they do not know they hasten their own trial.

mots.racine: س أ ل / د ي ن
mots.sens: they ask: when is the Day of Judgment?
mots.def: ayyāna: when; yawm al-dīn: Day of recompense.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ
versets.tr: yawma hum ʿalā n-nāri yuftanūna
versets.trad: On the Day they will be tried by the Fire [it will be said]:
versets.commentaire: “The Day they will be burned upon the Fire” (yuftanūn). The verb fitna, which denotes the testing of gold in the crucible, here means combustion. Qushayrī, in the inner reading, targets those who lie in their deeds through riyāʾ, in their states through self-satisfaction, and about God through the claims of states they ascribe to themselves.

mots.racine: ن و ر / ف ت ن
mots.sens: the day they are burned on the Fire

versets.ar: ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ هَٰذَا الَّذِي كُنتُم بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ
versets.tr: dhūqū fitnatakum hādhā lladhī kuntum bihi tastaʿjilūna
versets.trad: “Taste your trial! This is what you sought to hasten!”
versets.commentaire: “Taste your trial, this is what you sought to hasten.” Qushayrī: they will suffer the outcome of their disguise, deprived of breathing the fragrance of sincerity. What they demanded in mockery (verse 12) is served them: mocking impatience becomes torment tasted. The consequence is the very taste of the fault.

mots.racine: ذ و ق / ف ت ن
mots.sens: taste your trial

mots.racine: ع ج ل
mots.sens: this is what you sought to hasten

versets.ar: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ
versets.tr: inna l-muttaqīna fī jannātin wa-ʿuyūnin
versets.trad: The godfearing will be amid gardens and springs,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī opens the twofold abode: in their present, in the gardens of union; in their future, in the gardens of grace. Tomorrow: degrees and deliverance; today: nearnesses and confidings. What is deferred is the portion of their souls, what is advanced is the right of their Lord. The gardens and springs are at once future promise and present grace of the heart.

mots.racine: و ق ي / ج ن ن / ع ي ن
mots.sens: the godfearing are in gardens and springs

versets.ar: آخِذِينَ مَا آتَاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ
versets.tr: ākhidhīna mā ātāhum rabbuhum innahum kānū qabla dhālika muḥsinīna
versets.trad: receiving what their Lord gives them. Before that they were doers of good,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they take today what their Lord has given them, receiving it from the hand of gratitude and praise; tomorrow they will take what He bestows in Paradise. Whoever today receives from Him without intermediary — through faith, excellence (iḥsān), and the contemplation of the allotment in gift and deprivation — will likewise receive tomorrow, in Paradise, at the encounter and the face-to-face vision. He cites the hadith: “worship God as if you saw Him.”

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / أ ت ي
mots.sens: receiving what their Lord gave them
mots.def: ākhidh: who takes; ātā: to give.

mots.racine: ح س ن
mots.sens: before that they were doers of good
mots.def: muḥsin: who does good, the excellent.

versets.ar: كَانُوا قَلِيلًا مِّنَ اللَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ
versets.tr: kānū qalīlan mina l-layli mā yahjaʿūna
versets.trad: they slept little of the night,
versets.commentaire: They slept little of the night. Qushayrī evokes the lovers of the night: the night is, for the beloved, the intimacy of confiding, and for the sinners, the quest for salvation. Their vigil is constant: by excess of regret, by ardour of lack, by longing or by separation. He cites: “how many nights without dawn for me, I consumed them, my hand upon my liver…”

mots.ar: كَانُوا قَلِيلًا مِّنَ اللَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ
mots.tr: kānū qalīlan mina l-layli mā yahjaʿūn
mots.racine: ل ي ل / ه ج ع
mots.sens: they slept little of the night

versets.ar: وَبِالْأَسْحَارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-bi-l-asḥāri hum yastaghfirūna
versets.trad: and at the dawns they sought forgiveness.
versets.commentaire: At the last hours of night (asḥār), they seek forgiveness. Qushayrī: despite their vigil and prayer, they place themselves at dawn in the rank of sinners, imploring pardon, in contempt of their own worth and disdain of their act. Perfect humility: those who prayed most stand as the most at fault, asking for grace.

mots.racine: س ح ر / غ ف ر
mots.sens: at the dawns they implore forgiveness
mots.def: asḥār: last thirds of night; istaghfara: to implore forgiveness.

versets.ar: وَفِي أَمْوَالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِّلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ
versets.tr: wa-fī amwālihim ḥaqqun lil-sāʾili wa-l-maḥrūmi
versets.trad: In their wealth was a due for the beggar and the deprived.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the sāʾil is the one who holds out his hand; the maḥrūm, the one who refrains from asking — or who deprives himself by renouncing begging. These believers give according to the due. And he distinguishes the degrees: the people of magnanimity judge that the non-deserving has more right to their wealth than the deserving; and the people of destitution have no wealth left to give, for they have already preferred others to themselves in all.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / س أ ل / ح ر م
mots.sens: a due for the beggar and the deprived
mots.def: sāʾil: who asks; maḥrūm: deprived, the discreet needy.

versets.ar: وَفِي الْأَرْضِ آيَاتٌ لِّلْمُوقِنِينَ
versets.tr: wa-fī l-arḍi āyātun lil-mūqinīna
versets.trad: On the earth are signs for those of certainty,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī interiorizes the earth's signs through the portrait of the gnostic: just as the earth bears every thing, the gnostic bears everyone; whoever finds another a burden or is irked at seeing him, it is through absence of the Reality; and just as the earth receives every filth and brings forth only flowers, the gnostic absorbs every rudeness poured on him and exudes only noble character. He cites al-Junayd: “the Sufi is like the earth: every vile thing is cast on it, every fair thing comes out of it.”

mots.racine: أ ر ض / أ ي ي / ي ق ن
mots.sens: on the earth signs for the certain
mots.def: āyāt: signs; mūqin: who has certainty.

versets.ar: وَفِي أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-fī anfusikum a-fa-lā tubṣirūna
versets.trad: and in yourselves; do you not see?
versets.commentaire: “And in yourselves, do you not see?” Qushayrī applies this to the soul (nafs): among its signs: its insolence in its aspirations, its boldness in its qualities, its excessive claims about what it thinks it sees of itself and by itself, its sickly states when it ascribes to itself an atom of worth. The great sign is to discern in oneself the soul's ruses: to see oneself is already to begin to surpass oneself.

mots.racine: ن ف س
mots.sens: and in yourselves

mots.racine: ب ص ر
mots.sens: do you not see?

versets.ar: وَفِي السَّمَاءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-fī s-samāʾi rizqukum wa-mā tūʿadūna
versets.trad: And in the sky is your provision and what you are promised.
versets.commentaire: “In the sky, your provision and what you are promised.” Qushayrī: the allotment of your provisions is in the sky; or: provision comes by rain; or: your provision rests with the Lord of the sky, who has guaranteed it. And the pearl: in the sky your provision, and toward the sky rises your deed: if you want your provision to descend, send up your deed — which is why it is said: prayer knocks at the door of provision.

mots.racine: س م و / ر ز ق
mots.sens: in the sky your provision

mots.racine: و ع د
mots.sens: and what you are promised

versets.ar: فَوَرَبِّ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَا أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ
versets.tr: fa-wa-rabbi s-samāʾi wa-l-arḍi innahu la-ḥaqqun mithla mā annakum tanṭiqūna
versets.trad: By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is true, just as you are able to speak.
versets.commentaire: An oath by the Lord of the two worlds: the promise is true as your faculty of speech. Qushayrī: just as your word, none other utters it for you, your provision, none other eats it. And: He has placed your provision in the sky, and you have no way to ascend to it — so busy yourself with what you are charged with, and do not weary yourself seeking what you cannot reach.

mots.racine: ر ب ب
mots.sens: by the Lord of sky and earth

mots.ar: إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَا أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ
mots.tr: innahu la-ḥaqqun mithla mā annakum tanṭiqūn
mots.racine: ح ق ق / ن ط ق
mots.sens: it is true as your speaking

versets.ar: هَلْ أَتَاكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ
versets.tr: hal atāka ḥadīthu ḍayfi ibrāhīma l-mukramīna
versets.trad: Has the story of Abraham's honoured guests reached you?
versets.commentaire: The “honoured guests” (al-mukramūn) of Abraham. Qushayrī unfolds perfect hospitality: “honoured” because Abraham set himself to serve them; he honoured them by the serenity of his face and the joy of their coming; he did not make a show before them and did not apologize — that is honouring the guest, that there be no obligation weighing on the host which the guest must bear. And: among the noble, the unbidden guest is still honoured.

mots.racine: ح د ث / ض ي ف / ك ر م
mots.sens: the story of Abraham's honoured guests

versets.ar: إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا سَلَامًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَامٌ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ
versets.tr: idh dakhalū ʿalayhi fa-qālū salāman qāla salāmun qawmun munkarūna
versets.trad: When they entered upon him and said: “Peace!” He said: “Peace! A people unknown.”
versets.commentaire: “Peace!” — “Peace!” Qushayrī: salām means amān (security): we give you security, and from me you have peace. “A people unknown” (munkarūn): he did not know their like among guests; it is said: strangers. The exchange of peace between Abraham and the messenger-angels opens the scene of the annunciation.

mots.racine: س ل م
mots.sens: they said: peace! he said: peace

mots.racine: ن ك ر
mots.sens: a people unknown

versets.ar: فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِ فَجَاءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ
versets.tr: fa-rāgha ilā ahlihi fa-jāʾa bi-ʿijlin samīnin
versets.trad: Then he slipped away to his household and came with a fatted calf.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī dwells on “he slipped away (rāgha) to his household”: he stole away to them without their knowing — such is the slipping away of the noble: in secret, so as not to put the guest in difficulty. Abraham brings a fatted calf without noise or display: true generosity is accomplished in discretion, sparing the guest all embarrassment.

mots.racine: ر و غ
mots.sens: he slipped away to his household

mots.racine: ع ج ل / س م ن
mots.sens: he came with a fatted calf

versets.ar: فَقَرَّبَهُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ
versets.tr: fa-qarrabahu ilayhim qāla alā taʾkulūna
versets.trad: He set it near them and said: “Will you not eat?”
versets.commentaire: He roasted the calf, set it near them, and said: “Will you not eat?” Abraham's hospitality goes to the end of the gesture: not only does he serve, but he invites with delicacy. Their refusal to eat will, in the next verse, awaken his fear — for by custom, the guest who does not touch the meal is suspect.

mots.racine: ق ر ب
mots.sens: he set it near them

mots.racine: أ ك ل
mots.sens: he said: will you not eat?

versets.ar: فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ ۖ وَبَشَّرُوهُ بِغُلَامٍ عَلِيمٍ
versets.tr: fa-awjasa minhum khīfatan qālū lā takhaf wa-bashsharūhu bi-ghulāmin ʿalīmin
versets.trad: He felt some fear of them. They said: “Fear not!” and gave him good tidings of a learned boy.
themes: the fear and the good tidings
versets.commentaire: Seeing that they did not eat, Abraham felt a fear (khīfa) — he took them for brigands. Qushayrī: they said to him: “fear not,” and announced to him a boy — a son who would live until he became learned (ʿalīm). Dread turns to annunciation: the dreaded visit bore a promise. The ʿalīm is superlative — the child will become learned in growing.

mots.racine: و ج س / خ و ف
mots.sens: he felt a fear of them
mots.def: awjasa: to feel inwardly; khīfa: fear.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / غ ل م / ع ل م
mots.sens: they gave him good tidings of a learned boy

versets.ar: فَأَقْبَلَتِ امْرَأَتُهُ فِي صَرَّةٍ فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا وَقَالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ
versets.tr: fa-aqbalati mraʾatuhu fī ṣarratin fa-ṣakkat wajhahā wa-qālat ʿajūzun ʿaqīmun
versets.trad: His wife came forward with a cry; she struck her face and said: “A barren old woman?...”
versets.commentaire: Abraham's wife runs forward “in a cry” (ṣarra), strikes her face — a woman's gesture — and exclaims: “A barren old woman!” Qushayrī reports she was then ninety-eight and Abraham ninety-nine. Incredulity before the promise is not refusal but astonishment: the unheard-of exceeds understanding.

mots.racine: ق ب ل / ص ر ر
mots.sens: his wife came forward in a cry

mots.racine: ع ج ز / ع ق م
mots.sens: a barren old woman

versets.ar: قَالُوا كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ ۖ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ
versets.tr: qālū kadhāliki qāla rabbuki innahu huwa l-ḥakīmu l-ʿalīmu
versets.trad: They said: “So has your Lord said; He is the Wise, the All-Knowing!”
versets.commentaire: “So has your Lord said.” Qushayrī: He is the Wise (al-Ḥakīm) who masters His acts, the All-Knowing (al-ʿAlīm) from whom nothing escapes. The promise of the impossible rests on two Names: the wisdom that disposes and the knowledge that knows. What seems absurd to the human measure is perfectly ordered in the decree of the Wise.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ر ب ب
mots.sens: so has your Lord said

mots.racine: ح ك م / ع ل م
mots.sens: He is the Wise, the All-Knowing

versets.ar: قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ
versets.tr: qāla fa-mā khaṭbukum ayyuhā l-mursalūna
versets.trad: Abraham said: “O messengers, what then is your mission?”
versets.commentaire: Abraham, understanding they are messengers (mursalūn), inquires of their affair. Qushayrī: what is your case, your command, for what are you sent? The saint, once reassured, does not stop at his own joy: he inquires of the divine design these messengers bear — which will prove to be a punishment.

mots.racine: خ ط ب
mots.sens: what is your affair?

mots.racine: ر س ل
mots.sens: O messengers

versets.ar: قَالُوا إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنَا إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ
versets.tr: qālū innā ursilnā ilā qawmin mujrimīna
versets.trad: They said: “We have been sent to a criminal people,
versets.commentaire: “We have been sent to a criminal people” — that of Lot. The good tidings for Abraham are doubled by a threat for the perverse: the same messengers bear grace and punishment. The mujrim is one who has cut himself off from God by crime; the sending of the angels seals his fate.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ج ر م
mots.sens: we are sent to a criminal people

versets.ar: لِنُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن طِينٍ
versets.tr: li-nursila ʿalayhim ḥijāratan min ṭīnin
versets.trad: to send upon them stones of clay,
versets.commentaire: “To send upon them stones of clay” (ḥijāra min ṭīn). The punishment of Lot's people falls from the sky like a rain of hardened stones. Clay, from which man is drawn, becomes the instrument of his ruin: what forms can also annihilate, by the decree.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ح ج ر / ط ي ن
mots.sens: to send upon them stones of clay

versets.ar: مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ
versets.tr: musawwamatan ʿinda rabbika lil-musrifīna
versets.trad: marked by your Lord for the transgressors.”
versets.commentaire: These stones are “marked” (musawwama) with your Lord, destined for the excessive (musrifīn). Each stone bears its addressee: the punishment is not blind but adjusted. The musrif is one who has exceeded all measure; the mark on the stone speaks the precision of divine justice.

mots.racine: س و م
mots.sens: marked with your Lord

mots.racine: س ر ف
mots.sens: for the transgressors

versets.ar: فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَن كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-akhrajnā man kāna fīhā mina l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: So We brought out those of the believers who were there.
versets.commentaire: Before the punishment, God brings out the believers. Divine justice separates: no righteous one is swept away with the guilty. The salvation of the faithful precedes the annihilation of the perverse — proof that the punishment targets the crime, not the town itself.

mots.ar: فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَن كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
mots.tr: fa-akhrajnā man kāna fīhā mina l-muʾminīn
mots.racine: خ ر ج / أ م ن
mots.sens: We brought out the believers who were there
mots.def: akhraja: to bring out; muʾmin: believer.

versets.ar: فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِّنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-mā wajadnā fīhā ghayra baytin mina l-muslimīna
versets.trad: We found in it but one house of those submitted to God.
versets.commentaire: There was but one house of the submitted (muslimīn) — that of Lot and those who believed with him. The rarity underscores the moral wreck of the town: a single faithful household in a whole people. Yet this one household suffices to manifest that fidelity, even unique, is recognized and saved.

mots.racine: و ج د / ب ي ت
mots.sens: We found in it but one house

mots.racine: س ل م
mots.sens: of those submitted to God

versets.ar: وَتَرَكْنَا فِيهَا آيَةً لِّلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ الْعَذَابَ الْأَلِيمَ
versets.tr: wa-taraknā fīhā āyatan lilladhīna yakhāfūna l-ʿadhāba l-alīma
versets.trad: We left in it a sign for those who fear the painful punishment,
versets.commentaire: God has left a sign (āya) in the destroyed town. Qushayrī: a mark from which those who fear take a lesson — not the hardened hearts. The vestige of the ruin speaks only to whoever has kept fear: the same sign stays mute for the insensible heart and eloquent for the attentive one.

mots.racine: ت ر ك / أ ي ي
mots.sens: We left in it a sign

mots.racine: خ و ف / ع ذ ب / أ ل م
mots.sens: for those who fear the painful punishment

versets.ar: وَفِي مُوسَىٰ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَاهُ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطَانٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-fī mūsā idh arsalnāhu ilā firʿawna bi-sulṭānin mubīnin
versets.trad: And in Moses, when We sent him to Pharaoh with clear authority,
versets.commentaire: A new sign: Moses sent to Pharaoh “with a clear argument” (sulṭān mubīn). Qushayrī: a manifest, dazzling proof. The sulṭān here is not armed force but probative evidence: Moses comes unarmed, having for all power only the truth that sends him. And yet Pharaoh turns away.

mots.racine: ر س ل
mots.sens: when We sent him to Pharaoh

mots.racine: س ل ط / ب ي ن
mots.sens: with a clear argument

versets.ar: فَتَوَلَّىٰ بِرُكْنِهِ وَقَالَ سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ
versets.tr: fa-tawallā bi-ruknihi wa-qāla sāḥirun aw majnūnun
versets.trad: but he turned away with his might and said: “A magician or a madman!”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh “turned away with his support” (bi-ruknihi) — confident in his power and his armies — and called Moses a magician (sāḥir) or madman (majnūn). The same accusations levelled against Muḥammad (verse 52). The pride of earthly force refuses the evidence and brands as madness what surpasses it.

mots.racine: و ل ي / ر ك ن
mots.sens: he turned away with his support
mots.def: tawallā: to turn away; rukn: support, might.

mots.racine: س ح ر / ج ن ن
mots.sens: he said: a magician or madman

versets.ar: فَأَخَذْنَاهُ وَجُنُودَهُ فَنَبَذْنَاهُمْ فِي الْيَمِّ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ
versets.tr: fa-akhadhnāhu wa-junūdahu fa-nabadhnāhum fī l-yammi wa-huwa mulīmun
versets.trad: So We seized him and his armies and cast them into the sea, he being blameworthy.
versets.commentaire: God seized Pharaoh and his armies and cast them into the waters (yamm), he “blaming himself” (mulīm) — deserving reproach. The power on which he prided himself (verse 39) does not save him: the earthly support collapses in the abyss. Pharaoh's end is the counter-example to the salvation of Lot's believers.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ج ن د
mots.sens: We seized him and his armies

mots.racine: ن ب ذ / ي م م / ل و م
mots.sens: We cast them into the sea, blameworthy
mots.def: yamm: sea; mulīm: who deserves blame.

versets.ar: وَفِي عَادٍ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّيحَ الْعَقِيمَ
versets.tr: wa-fī ʿādin idh arsalnā ʿalayhimu r-rīḥa l-ʿaqīma
versets.trad: And in ʿĀd, when We sent against them the barren wind
versets.commentaire: The sign of ʿĀd: the “barren wind” (al-rīḥ al-ʿaqīm). Barren, for it carries neither rain nor fecundity, but only destruction. The same wind that elsewhere scatters the beneficent clouds (verse 1) becomes here an instrument of punishment: creation obeys, by the decree, grace as well as rigour.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ر و ح / ع ق م
mots.sens: when We sent the barren wind

versets.ar: مَا تَذَرُ مِن شَيْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَالرَّمِيمِ
versets.tr: mā tadharu min shayʾin atat ʿalayhi illā jaʿalathu ka-r-ramīmi
versets.trad: leaving nothing it came upon without making it like crumbled ruin.
versets.commentaire: The wind leaves nothing without reducing it “like crumbled bone” (ramīm). The devastation is total: all it touches falls to debris. An image of the absolute impotence of ʿĀd, once so robust, before the breath of the decree: physical power is nothing against the divine order.

mots.ar: مَا تَذَرُ مِن شَيْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ
mots.tr: mā tadharu min shayʾin atat ʿalayhi
mots.racine: و ذ ر / أ ت ي
mots.sens: it leaves nothing it comes upon

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ر م م
mots.sens: without making it like crumbled bone

versets.ar: وَفِي ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-fī thamūda idh qīla lahum tamattaʿū ḥattā ḥīnin
versets.trad: And in Thamūd, when it was said to them: “Enjoy yourselves for a time!”
versets.commentaire: The sign of Thamūd: “Enjoy yourselves until a term.” The granted respite is no grace but an ultimate warning: a delay to recover. But enjoyment without repentance leads to punishment. The fixed “term” (ḥīn) recalls that every delay has its end, and that heedlessness in the respite worsens the fault.

mots.ar: إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ
mots.tr: idh qīla lahum tamattaʿū ḥattā ḥīn
mots.racine: م ت ع / ح ي ن
mots.sens: when it was said: enjoy until a term
mots.def: tamattaʿa: to enjoy; ḥīn: term, time.

versets.ar: فَعَتَوْا عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ
versets.tr: fa-ʿataw ʿan amri rabbihim fa-akhadhathumu ṣ-ṣāʿiqatu wa-hum yanẓurūna
versets.trad: They defied the command of their Lord, so the thunderbolt seized them as they looked on.
versets.commentaire: They rebelled (ʿataw) against their Lord's command, and the thunderbolt (ṣāʿiqa) seized them “as they looked on” (yanẓurūn) — in full awareness, with no escape. The punishment comes openly, in broad day, as if to make blindness impossible. Revolt summons the thunderbolt; the scorned respite closes.

mots.racine: ع ت و
mots.sens: they rebelled against their Lord's command

mots.racine: ص ع ق / ن ظ ر
mots.sens: the thunderbolt seized them as they looked

versets.ar: فَمَا اسْتَطَاعُوا مِن قِيَامٍ وَمَا كَانُوا مُنتَصِرِينَ
versets.tr: fa-mā staṭāʿū min qiyāmin wa-mā kānū muntaṣirīna
versets.trad: They could neither rise nor defend themselves.
versets.commentaire: They could neither rise (qiyām) nor defend themselves (muntaṣirīn). The collapse is without recourse: neither the strength to get up, nor the help to protect oneself. It is the sūra's recurrent image — no refuge outside God: whoever rises against the divine order finds himself without support or defender.

mots.racine: ط و ع / ق و م
mots.sens: they could not rise
mots.def: istaṭāʿa: to be able; qiyām: standing.

mots.racine: ن ص ر
mots.sens: they could not defend themselves

versets.ar: وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَوْمًا فَاسِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-qawma nūḥin min qablu innahum kānū qawman fāsiqīna
versets.trad: And the people of Noah before: they were a perverse people.
versets.commentaire: The people of Noah, more ancient still, were “a perverse people” (fāsiqīn). The litany of punished peoples — Lot, Pharaoh, ʿĀd, Thamūd, Noah — runs back in time to show one constant law: perversity (fisq), departure from obedience, leads to ruin. History only repeats the same warning.

mots.sens: and the people of Noah before

mots.racine: ف س ق
mots.sens: they were a perverse people

versets.ar: وَالسَّمَاءَ بَنَيْنَاهَا بِأَيْدٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-s-samāʾa banaynāhā bi-aydin wa-innā la-mūsiʿūna
versets.trad: And the sky We built with might, and We are its expanders.
versets.commentaire: After the peoples, the signs of the cosmos. “The sky, We built it with might (bi-ayd), and We are the expanders (mūsiʿūn).” Qushayrī: We made between it and the earth a vast expanse, and We are able to increase that amplitude still. The power that punishes is the same that builds: the Master of punishment is the Master of creation.

mots.racine: س م و / ب ن ي / أ ي د
mots.sens: the sky, We built it with might

mots.racine: و س ع
mots.sens: We are the expanders

versets.ar: وَالْأَرْضَ فَرَشْنَاهَا فَنِعْمَ الْمَاهِدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-l-arḍa farashnāhā fa-niʿma l-māhidūna
versets.trad: And the earth We spread out — how excellent are the Spreaders!
versets.commentaire: “The earth, We spread it out like a carpet; how excellent the preparers (al-māhidūn)!” Qushayrī: We made it a cradle for you — then God praises Himself for His own work. All this attests, he says, the perfection of His power and the fullness of His grace and mercy.

mots.racine: أ ر ض / ف ر ش
mots.sens: the earth, We spread it out
mots.def: farasha: to spread like a carpet.

mots.racine: م ه د
mots.sens: how excellent the preparers

versets.ar: وَمِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنَا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-min kulli shayʾin khalaqnā zawjayni laʿallakum tadhakkarūna
versets.trad: Of every thing We created pairs, that you may reflect.
versets.commentaire: “Of every thing We created a pair (zawjayn).” Qushayrī: two kinds — in the living, male and female; elsewhere, motion and rest, black and white, and all the pairs of opposites. The duality of the created points back to the Oneness of the Creator: all that is in pairs points toward the One who alone has no like.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ز و ج
mots.sens: of every thing We created a pair

mots.racine: ذ ك ر
mots.sens: that you may reflect

versets.ar: فَفِرُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّي لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: fa-firrū ilā llāhi innī lakum minhu nadhīrun mubīnun
versets.trad: So flee to God! Truly, I am to you from Him a clear warner.
versets.commentaire: A summit-verse: “Flee to God!” (firrū ilā llāh). Qushayrī unfolds this flight: man must flee from ignorance to knowledge, from passion to piety, from doubt to certainty, from the devil to God; flee from his own act — which is his trial — to His act — which is his sufficiency; from His attribute that is wrath to His attribute that is mercy; from Him — “God warns you of Himself” — to Him — “flee to God.” And: whoever makes pure his flight to God makes pure his dwelling with God.

mots.racine: ف ر ر
mots.sens: flee to God

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / ب ي ن
mots.sens: I am from Him a clear warner to you

versets.ar: وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلَٰهًا آخَرَ ۖ إِنِّي لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: wa-lā tajʿalū maʿa llāhi ilāhan ākhara innī lakum minhu nadhīrun mubīnun
versets.trad: And do not set up another god beside God! Truly, I am to you from Him a clear warner.
versets.commentaire: To the flight to God answers the prohibition of associating: set no other deity beside Him. The flight has meaning only toward the One: to take refuge with another would be to flee the refuge itself. The repetition of “I am to you a clear warner” seals the urgency: tawḥīd is the term of all flight.

mots.ar: وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلَٰهًا آخَرَ
mots.tr: wa-lā tajʿalū maʿa llāhi ilāhan ākhar
mots.racine: ج ع ل / أ ل ه
mots.sens: set no other deity beside God

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / ب ي ن
mots.sens: a clear warner

versets.ar: كَذَٰلِكَ مَا أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا قَالُوا سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ
versets.tr: kadhālika mā atā lladhīna min qablihim min rasūlin illā qālū sāḥirun aw majnūnun
versets.trad: Thus no messenger came to those before them but they said: “A magician or a madman!”
versets.commentaire: The same accusation runs through history: no prophet came without being called “a magician or a madman” — exactly what Pharaoh says of Moses (verse 39). The rejection of the Prophet is thus no singularity but the repetition of a pattern: the deniers of every age speak the same language.

mots.ar: مَا أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ
mots.tr: mā atā lladhīna min qablihim min rasūl
mots.racine: أ ت ي / ر س ل
mots.sens: no messenger came to those before them

mots.racine: س ح ر / ج ن ن
mots.sens: but they said: a magician or madman

versets.ar: أَتَوَاصَوْا بِهِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ
versets.tr: a-tawāṣaw bihi bal hum qawmun ṭāghūna
versets.trad: Did they bequeath this to one another? Indeed, they are a rebellious people!
versets.commentaire: “Did they hand this watchword to one another?” Qushayrī: as if they had mutually recommended it. But the true cause is no transmission: they are a rebellious people (ṭāghūn). The constancy of the accusation across the ages comes not from a plot but from one root: the excess (ṭughyān) of the heart that refuses to submit.

mots.racine: و ص ي
mots.sens: did they recommend it to one another?

mots.racine: ط غ ي
mots.sens: they are a rebellious people

versets.ar: فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَمَا أَنتَ بِمَلُومٍ
versets.tr: fa-tawalla ʿanhum fa-mā anta bi-malūmin
versets.trad: So turn away from them; you are not blameworthy.
versets.commentaire: “Turn away from them; you are not blameworthy.” Qushayrī: withdraw: no reproach reaches you for their misconduct. The Prophet has transmitted; the refusal binds only the refusers. To turn away is not to abandon the mission but to recognize the heart's freedom: the warner is not accountable for the listener's obstinacy.

mots.racine: و ل ي
mots.sens: turn away from them

mots.racine: ل و م
mots.sens: you are not blameworthy

versets.ar: وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ الذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: wa-dhakkir fa-inna dh-dhikrā tanfaʿu l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: And remind, for the reminder benefits the believers.
versets.commentaire: “Remind, for the reminder benefits the believers.” Qushayrī unfolds the recipients: remind the sinners of My punishment, that they may return; remind the obedient of My abundant reward, that they may grow in obedience; remind the gnostics of what I have warded off from them of My trial; remind the rich of My bounties; remind the poor of what I have turned away from them of this world and what I have prepared for them of My encounter.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر
mots.sens: remind

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ن ف ع / أ م ن
mots.sens: the reminder benefits the believers

versets.ar: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ
versets.tr: wa-mā khalaqtu l-jinna wa-l-insa illā li-yaʿbudūni
versets.trad: I created the jinn and humankind only that they may worship Me.
versets.commentaire: The great verse of purpose: “I created jinn and humankind only that they may worship Me.” Qushayrī illumines it with a reading of the decree: those whom I elected from eternity, distinguished today by the beauty of My welcome, to whom I promised My immense favours — I created them only that they may worship Me. Worship (ʿibāda) is no imposed drudgery but the very reason for being of the elect, his highest vocation.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ج ن ن / أ ن س
mots.sens: I created jinn and humankind

mots.racine: ع ب د
mots.sens: only that they worship Me

versets.ar: مَا أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ
versets.tr: mā urīdu minhum min rizqin wa-mā urīdu an yuṭʿimūni
versets.trad: I want no provision from them, nor do I want them to feed Me.
versets.commentaire: God asks of creatures neither provision nor food: worship is no service He needs. Qushayrī: I do not want them to feed or provide for any of My servants, for the Provider is I. Worship is entirely for the servant's good: God, the absolute Rich, draws nothing from the worship He commands.

mots.racine: ر و د / ر ز ق
mots.sens: I want no provision from them

mots.racine: ط ع م
mots.sens: I do not want them to feed Me

versets.ar: إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ
versets.tr: inna llāha huwa r-razzāqu dhū l-quwwati l-matīnu
versets.trad: Truly, God is the Provider, the Possessor of strength, the Firm.
versets.commentaire: Three Names seal the purpose: al-Razzāq (the supreme Provider), Dhū l-quwwa (Possessor of strength), al-Matīn (the Unshakable). Since it is He who provides, man need not burden himself with his sustenance but with his worship. God's unshakable strength guarantees that none will lack what is apportioned to him.

mots.racine: ر ز ق
mots.sens: God is the supreme Provider

mots.racine: ق و ي / م ت ن
mots.sens: Possessor of strength, the Unshakable

versets.ar: فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ذَنُوبًا مِّثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحَابِهِمْ فَلَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ
versets.tr: fa-inna lilladhīna ẓalamū dhanūban mithla dhanūbi aṣḥābihim fa-lā yastaʿjilūni
versets.trad: To those who do wrong belongs a share like that of their fellows; so let them not ask Me to hasten.
versets.commentaire: The unjust will have a share (dhanūb — lit. a bucket) of punishment like that of their perished fellows. Qushayrī: why hasten the punishment, when it will not escape them? Their mocking impatience (verse 12) returns: what they demand will surely come; no need to hasten it, for the decree is inevitable.

mots.racine: ذ ن ب
mots.sens: a share like that of their fellows
mots.def: dhanūb: a great bucket, a share.

mots.racine: ع ج ل
mots.sens: let them not hasten Me

versets.ar: فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِن يَوْمِهِمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: fa-waylun lilladhīna kafarū min yawmihimu lladhī yūʿadūna
versets.trad: Woe to the disbelievers from their Day which they are promised!
versets.commentaire: The sūra closes on the woe (wayl) promised to the disbelievers on the announced Day — the Day of Resurrection. The whole course of the sūra leads back to it: from the oath by the winds to the purpose of worship, the promise is true (verse 5) and the reckoning will take place. He who refused to flee to God (verse 50) meets, in the end, the Day he had mocked.

mots.racine: ك ف ر
mots.sens: woe to the disbelievers

mots.racine: و ع د
mots.sens: from their Day which they are promised
